**`DECISIONS 2026-06-09 (capability survey S2)` ŌĆö The required-capability catalog is harvested from the flow shape, reconciled against the actual grant, and held total by `required Ōćö surveyed`.**

S2 of the capability survey (`HANDOFF_CAPABILITY_SURVEY_2026_06_09.md`). The S1 prototype reconciled each environment's *declared* `grant` facet against its *actual* grant. S2 resolves the handoff's open-Q1 (coarse facet vs per-use-case union) by shifting the survey to the operator's actual question ŌĆö *"all the activities required by the use cases in the pipeline ŌĆö we preflight those"* ŌĆö and the trigger has fired: a model / no-data flow against a `schema+data` target needs `ALTER`/`CREATE TABLE` but no `INSERT`/`DELETE`, so the coarse declared-vs-actual reconciliation would **over-refuse**.

The substantive resolutions:

1. **A `Capability` vocabulary, derived from the flow shape, not a protein classifier.** `CapabilitySurvey.Capability = Reads | Performs of Preflight.WriteAction` ŌĆö the **source** role reads (`SELECT`); the **sink** role performs the writes the flow's scope + data origin exercise. `requiredBy : ProjectionConfig -> Flow -> SubstrateRole -> Set<Capability>` derives each flow's requirement purely from its shape (target `grant` + source kind), faithful to `Command.planMovement`'s routing (data-only target ŌåÆ the data leg; schema-bearing target + live source ŌåÆ `MigrateWithData`; schema-bearing target + model/no-data ŌåÆ schema only). The obligations matrix (`THE_USE_CASE_ONTOLOGY.obligations.md` ┬¦3/┬¦6 AC-G1/AC-G2 ŌĆö the gate plane) is the spec the derivation checks against, not a classifier to build.

2. **The required set is HARVESTED per place** (`requiredOf`): the union of what every configured flow asks of an environment across every role it plays (`touchedBy` binds `to`ŌåÆSink, live `from`ŌåÆSource). The survey reconciles this harvested union against the actual `fn_my_permissions` grant, **subsuming** the coarse declared facet. An environment no flow touches is asked for nothing (vacuously covered) ŌĆö the declared `grant` facet is now a derivation *input* (target grant ŌåÆ permitted sink writes via `requiredFor`), not a standalone assertion.

3. **`required Ōćö surveyed` is total by construction** (`CapabilitySurveyTotalityTests`) ŌĆö the sibling of the transform registry's `registered Ōćö executed` and the voice `code Ōćö copy`. The structural half is the compiler's: `Capability.permissionOf` / `surveyedBy` are total over the closed `Capability` DU (and `Capability.all` is derived from `Preflight.allWriteActions`, never a parallel list), so a new capability cannot land without a probe. The test pins both directions (every required capability is probeable; no dead probe) plus the coarse-upper-bound invariant `requiredBy(sink) ŌŖå requiredFor(targetGrant)` that locates exactly where S2 earns its place (the proper-subset witness).

4. **Read-only held; pure-Core held; no new probe.** The reconciliation is pure; the only I/O is the boundary `survey`. Source-read (`SELECT`) reuses the read-only `fn_my_permissions` evidence the survey already captures ŌĆö S2 adds a reconciliation axis, not a probe. `Preflight.permissionName` is now public + `coversPermissionAtDatabaseScope` factors the by-permission check so the survey names `SELECT` in the same vocabulary the writes are probed under. The `EnvironmentReport.Missing` field is now `Capability list` (IR grows under evidence ŌĆö the report carries `Reads` alongside the writes); `EnvironmentReport.Required` carries the harvested set.

Full pure pool green (2910 passed / 0 failed / 208 skip); 17 survey tests + 13 ViewTests + 18 PreflightTests green. **Deferred to S3 (operator decision owed):** survey-as-mandatory-G0-pre-flight is gated on the gate-vs-advisory call (R6 ŌĆö advisory until the per-pair flip); the object-scope grant refinement + permission-vocabulary confirmation (S4) waits on a real managed UAT login.

---

**`DECISIONS 2026-06-09 (capability survey S3 posture)` ŌĆö The survey-as-G0 pre-flight is ADVISORY until the per-pair V2-driver flip; the hard gate is not the default.**

The operator decision the S2 handoff owed before S3 (wiring the survey as the mandatory G0 pre-flight ŌĆö closing the obligations doc's master gap, `Preflight.all` with zero callers). **Resolved: advisory until the per-pair flip.** When S3 wires the survey into the run verbs' pre-flight (MC/MX/TR), an under-capable connected place is **surfaced** (the survey renders the missing capabilities; `projection survey` keeps its non-zero advisory exit 7), but a live run still **proceeds** ŌĆö the hard refusal arrives per-environment-per-artifact-type, with the V2-driver flip, not as a blanket G0 stop.

The rationale is R6 (`DECISIONS 2026-05-22 ŌĆö R6: split-brain governance`): V2 owns no production write path during the dual-track window ŌĆö V1 drives, the canary asserts V1 Ōēł V2. A survey-driven hard stop on a run V1 still owns would be V2 seizing a gate it does not yet hold. The per-pair V2-driver transition (gated on N=10 green canary runs + operator sign-off) is where the survey's advisory verdict graduates to a hard refusal, for that pair only ŌĆö never global, never a default. The hard gate ("hard gate now") and the per-pair config-armed gate were both considered and declined: the first contradicts R6; the second adds a config axis ahead of a consumer (IR grows under evidence).

So S3's shape: `Preflight.all` becomes the single mandatory composition the verbs route through (closing AC-G0's `built-not-wired` master gap), the survey feeds it, and the composition reports the refusal **advisory** ŌĆö the operator sees exactly which place is under-capable before the run, and the run proceeds under V1's ownership. The flip from advisory to hard is a per-pair operator action (R6), recorded when it fires.

---

## 2026-06-09 ŌĆö The three cloud-insertion data producers (`synthetic` / `legacy` / `peer`); A and B are dispositions, not times; `golden` re-keys users

Resolved a multi-turn operator co-design of the **data origins that feed cloud insertion** ŌĆö writing production-like rows *up* into a live cloud OutSystems environment (`cloud-uat`, a `direct` sink with `grant: data`, DML-only). Captured in the new design doc **`THE_DATA_PRODUCERS.md`** (sibling to `THE_SYNTHETIC_DATA_DESIGN.md` + `PRESCOPE_TRANSFER.md`); this entry is the resolved-questions record.

**1. The producer trinity is the cut.** Three origins feed cloud insertion, distinguished by *what the origin IS* and *which rendition it bears* ŌĆö all three move the **same `SsKey` model** (none is foreign schema): **`synthetic`** (data generated to match a captured `Profile`, `Žā : Profile Ō¤Č Data` ŌĆö **BUILT**, `THE_SYNTHETIC_DATA_DESIGN.md`); **`legacy`** (the **BŌåÆA reverse leg** ŌĆö the logical on-prem rendition the migration team populated, piped back up into the physical cloud; the `preview` flow); **`peer`** (a **peer cell at the same (physical) rendition** ŌĆö e.g. `cloud-qa`; same model, only surrogates differ; the `golden` flow). The `legacy`Ōåö`peer` distinction is the **source's rendition** (logical reverse-leg vs same-rendition physical peer), not foreign-vs-same schema. Identity is reconciled the same way for both (by `SsKey` / business key ŌĆö it is the same model); `peer` re-keys because surrogates differ across cells, `legacy` reconciles on the reverse leg. *(Item 1's earlier "legacy = foreign schema, identity must be discovered" framing was wrong ŌĆö see the course correction below.)*

**2. `peer` is the concept-shaped name** for what `THE_SYNTHETIC_DATA_DESIGN.md` ┬¦1 called **`sibling-cloud`**. Per the domain-first naming discipline (`CLAUDE.md` pillar 8): the load-bearing property ŌĆö the reason the simpler identity treatment applies ŌĆö is "a peer cell of the same estate lattice" (`THE_USE_CASE_ONTOLOGY.md` ┬¦3 lattice vocabulary). The rename is **propagated this commit** to `THE_SYNTHETIC_DATA_DESIGN.md` ┬¦1 + `THE_CLI.md` ┬¦1. ("sibling" was the operator's first reach; "peer" + the lattice "cell" framing was chosen over `estate-peer` / `sibling-cell`.)

**3. `golden` = exclude user rows, re-key their FKs.** The `peer`/`golden` flow does **not** copy `dbo.User` rows; the sink keeps its own users. Every FK referencing a user (`CreatedBy`/`UpdatedBy`/any `dbo.User` ref) on the copied rows is **Reidentified** to the sink's matching user surrogate, reconciled by **business key (email)** ŌĆö the `ByEmail` rule of P-3 (`THE_USE_CASE_ONTOLOGY.md` ┬¦3). Gated by `validate-user-map` **before any DML**: an unmapped orphan fails loud (no silent `NULL`, no `FallbackToSystemUser` unless declared). A re-keyed row is an **Update**, never Delete+Insert (P-REKEY). This is the same protocol as the `uat` DevŌåÆUAT re-key, applied cloudŌåÆcloud within one estate.

**4. A and B are two dispositions of one model, not two points in time.** `A` = the **physical `OSUSR_*` rendition** (the cloud OutSystems realization; `Realization = physical`); `B` = the **on-prem schema-migration target** (`Realization = logical`). Same `SsKey` identity, same model, same concern ŌĆö two realizations; B-shaped data is "more production-like" only because B is the migration target. This **identifies the `THE_USE_CASE_ONTOLOGY.md` ┬¦3 Realization name-space with the `WAVE_6_ALGEBRA.md` torsor's A/B**: cloud insertion is the *up* leg `emit(B ŌŖ¢ A)` rendering disposition A; on-prem projection renders disposition B. The consequence: physical/logical realization is not "steady-state policy + an out-of-scope cutover" but **the two first-class dispositions the bidirectional model moves between** ŌĆö and a producer generates *the model's data*, with the disposition being only the realization the chosen sink uses.

**5. Validation is a data-level canary per producer** (the data extension of H-050, `PRESCOPE_TRANSFER.md` North Star; siblings of the schema canary P-9). `synthetic` ŌåÆ `ŽĆ Ōłś Žā Ōēł id` (`SyntheticCanaryTests`, **green**); `legacy` ŌåÆ the **BŌåÆA reverse-leg round-trip** (logical on-prem ŌåÆ physical cloud ŌåÆ readback; same model, **no foreign tolerances**); `peer` ŌåÆ the re-key canary (the `Order ŌåÆ User-by-email` join identical across source and sink while source user surrogates are provably absent from the sink). All three are preview/migration tooling inside R6 ŌĆö never a production write path; every live write needs `--go` + `PROJECTION_ALLOW_EXECUTE`.

**Corpus propagation (this commit).** The crystallized understanding is propagated across the load-bearing surfaces so the corpus is consistent and execute-ready: `THE_USE_CASE_ONTOLOGY.md` (┬¦5.8 ŌĆö Realization is a *disposition*, not a constant, with A=physical-`OSUSR_*` / B=logical-on-prem; ┬¦3 intro disposition reading; P-3 names the cloudŌåÆcloud `golden` instance; ┬¦6.2 corpus-map row), `WAVE_6_ALGEBRA.md` ┬¦1 (A/B-as-dispositions note on the carrier table ŌĆö the torsor axioms unchanged, A/B are points either way), `WAVE_6_ONTOLOGY.md` (the lattice-cell line gains the disposition reading), `PRESCOPE_TRANSFER.md` ┬¦0 (producer trinity + dispositions cross-cut), `TRANSFER_ISOMORPHISM_SUBSTANTIATION.md` ┬¦1 (per-producer canary note), `THE_CLI.md` ┬¦1 (producer trinity + `golden` annotation), `THE_SYNTHETIC_DATA_DESIGN.md` ┬¦1 (the rename). `THE_DATA_PRODUCERS.md` gained ┬¦5ŌĆō┬¦6 (reusable-machinery anchors + per-producer build slices PE-1..3 / LE-1..2 + acceptance) so `legacy` and `peer` are buildable to the depth `synthetic` already holds. `WAVE_6_MORPHOLOGY.md` (a frozen as-is snapshot) and `AXIOMS.md` (the torsor axioms are unchanged) are deliberately untouched.

**Course correction (2026-06-09, later) ŌĆö `legacy` is the BŌåÆA reverse leg, not foreign schema; `rendition` env flag.** The operator corrected two things before any implementation. (a) **`legacy` is NOT foreign schema.** The flow: the cloud schema is exported into the logical model and hosted on-prem (rendition B); the **migration team loads the data into that logical model** (the foreignŌåÆlogical mapping is *theirs*, upstream of the engine); then the **same model in reverse** pipes the data back up into the physical cloud model (rendition A, the "frozen physical / `OSUSR_*`" version). So `legacy` is a **same-`SsKey`-model logicalŌåÆphysical (BŌåÆA) reverse leg** ŌĆö the engine never sees foreign schema, there is no foreign-ingest milestone and no translation tolerance. The earlier "foreign-schema substrate mapped through" framing (items 1 + 5 above, now corrected inline) was wrong. (b) **The producer-modelling fork is settled as an env-metadata flag `rendition: physical | logical`** (operator chose the flag over `FlowSource` variants; "estate" was rejected as the name). Each environment is marked with the rendition it bears; the flag distinguishes a `peer` source (physical) from a `legacy` source (logical) and tells the engine the move's direction + which rendition to read/emit. (c) A consequence surfaced: **emitting/matching the physical `OSUSR_*` rendition for a cloud sink** is the genuine shared new engine work (the engine targets the logical rendition today, `Realization := Designation`) ŌĆö tracked as PREFLIGHT M1.5 / `CONFIRMED_BACKLOG` J1 / risk R-5. **Re-corrected this commit:** `THE_DATA_PRODUCERS.md` (rewritten ŌĆö producer cut by source rendition, the BŌåÆA reverse leg, the `rendition` decision ┬¦4.6, ┬¦5/┬¦6 anchors + slices), `PREFLIGHT_CLOUD_INSERTION.md` (M1=rendition flag, new M1.5, M3=reverse leg, acceptance + risks D-1/R-3/R-5), `CONFIRMED_BACKLOG` ┬¦J (J1/J3 + gate), `PRESCOPE_TRANSFER.md` ┬¦0, `TRANSFER_ISOMORPHISM_SUBSTANTIATION.md` ┬¦1, `THE_CLI.md` ┬¦1. **Implementation deferred** until the course-correction is complete across all plans/docs (operator instruction); M2 (the `peer`/`golden` cloudŌåÆcloud re-key canary) is the agreed first build slice once it resumes.

No code or events changed ŌĆö this is design grounding for the cloud-insertion producer layer (the engine machinery exists: `DataOrigin`/`FlowSource`/`Rekey`/`Reconcile`/`Tables` on `MovementSpec`, the Transfer write seam, the P-3 re-key chain). Tree green at entry time.

## 2026-06-09 ŌĆö M1.5 probe + M2 shipped (golden cloudŌåÆcloud re-key; two transfer correctness fixes)

**M1.5 probe ŌĆö supersedes item (c) above.** A code probe (`TransferRun.fs:909-918`) corrected the physical-rendition framing: there is **no `OSUSR_*` name generator and none is needed** ŌĆö physical names are read from a real DB via `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` (`ReadSide`). The transfer writes using the **source** contract's names (the `LogicalTableEmission` passes are SSDT-DDL-only, not in the transfer path), so physical `OSUSR_*` names round-trip as-read (`TransferCanaryTests` deploys OSUSR source+sink green). The real M1.5 gap is narrow and **scoped to `legacy`**: cross-rendition write-target resolution ŌĆö a logical-sourceŌåÆphysical-sink (BŌåÆA) leg must resolve each write target per-`SsKey` against the **sink** catalog. `peer` (AŌåÆA, same rendition) needs none, so **M2 was reachable without M1.5**. Corrected across PREFLIGHT / `THE_DATA_PRODUCERS` / `CONFIRMED_BACKLOG`.

**M2 shipped ŌĆö `peer`/`golden` cloudŌåÆcloud re-key canary, green.** Built directly on the Transfer engine + reconcile (the offline canary uses two OSUSR-named ephemeral DBs, bypassing the M1 env flag). Two **production correctness fixes** in `TransferRun.fs`, both surfaced *during* the build and squarely the golden "exclude users, re-key their FKs" contract:
1. **The wipe never deletes a `ReconciledByRule` kind.** Extracted the pure `Transfer.wipeTargets` from `wipeFkOrdered`; a reconciled kind's sink rows are the sink's OWN (matched by email), so a `WipeAndLoad` refresh that `DELETE`d them ŌĆö with the zeroed plan never re-inserting ŌĆö would have destroyed the sink's user inventory. Also respects the golden `tables` LoadSet (an excluded kind is untouched, not wiped). Threaded the LoadSet through `wipeFkOrdered` + `writePlanResumable`; `runSynthetic` passes `None`.
2. **The LoadSet filter keeps reconcile-source kinds.** `reconcileAgainstSink` reads source rows from the LoadSet-filtered map, so `tables=[Order]` had silently starved the User email remap. The filter now unions the reconciled kinds back in; the plan still zeroes them (`reclassifyReconciled`), so they remain never-inserted while the FK re-key resolves.

Witnesses: `TransferRefusalTests` `PE-1` ├Ś3 (pure ŌĆö `wipeTargets` excludes ReconciledByRule + respects LoadSet); `TransferCanaryTests` `PE-2 golden` (Docker ŌĆö unmapped user FK halts before any DML, AC-I5) + `PE-3 golden` (Docker ŌĆö `tables=[Order]` + reconcile User by email: the OrderŌåÆUser-by-email join is identical srcŌåösink, the source user surrogates are absent from the sink, User is `ReconciledByRule` `RowsWritten=0`, the sink's own inventory unchanged; P-REKEY/AC-I2). Green: `TransferRefusalTests` 16/16 + full Docker pool 177/177 (no regression in the DevŌåÆUAT re-key or the 4.2 apparatus canary). **Follow-on (J2):** wire the `golden` CLI flow to *default* a `User:Email` reconcile from config (`resolveFlowSpec`) ŌĆö today it needs an explicit `--reconcile` tail.

## 2026-06-09 ŌĆö M1.5 + M3 shipped (legacy BŌåÆA reverse leg; the two-contract path IS the mechanism)

**Verdict (corrects the M2 probe's surface reading):** the legacy BŌåÆA reverse leg needs **no new write-path code** ŌĆö the engine half was already built as the **6.B.2 two-contract rename-aware path**. `Transfer.runWithRenames` / `runReconcilingWithRenames` (`TransferRun.fs:782,833`) pass the **sink** contract as `runCore`'s `catalog` and `(sourceContract, renameMap)` as the `ingestion` tuple: ingestion reads the source (logical) DB with the source contract's `kind.Physical` names; `RenameProjection.repointRows` re-keys each row's column-values onto the sink's column names by SsKey; the plan is built against the **sink** contract, so the write seam targets the sink's `kind.Physical` (the OSUSR table). **Both table-name AND column-name rendition resolve by SsKey** ŌĆö table by per-SsKey plan binding, column by the rename map. The M2 probe's "writes using the source contract's names (`TransferRun.fs:909`)" was the *non-rename* AŌåÆA path (where source and sink share one `catalog`); the two-contract path is the corrected mechanism and routes the write through the sink contract. There is no `OSUSR_*` name generator and none is needed (physical names are read from the real DB).

**M3 / LE-2 green.** `TransferCanaryTests` `M3/LE-2` (Docker): authored `legacySourceContract` (logical `[dbo].[Customer].[Email]`) + `legacySinkContract` (physical `[dbo].[OSUSR_XF_CUSTOMER].[CONTACT]`) share kind+attribute SsKeys but differ in BOTH table and column name; seed the logical source, `runWithRenames` up into the empty OSUSR sink, assert `CONTACT` carries the source `EMAIL` data exactly (no foreign tolerances ŌĆö same model). Full canary class 20/20.

**Load-bearing constraint ŌĆö SsKeys are name-derived in `ReadSide`** (`kindSsKey`/`attributeSsKey` synthesize from schema.table[.column], `ReadSide.fs:68-75`). So two independently-read DBs (a logical B + a physical A) would produce DISJOINT SsKeys and could not align ŌĆö the LE-2 fixture therefore uses **authored** contracts with stable SsKeys (the rename-canary precedent), NOT ReadSide reconstruction. Implication for the *real* flow: it must thread stable SsKeys across both renditions ŌĆö either a **shared authored model** rendered twice (the migration-team case: their model is authored, not discovered ŌĆö the near-term answer), or **attribute-scope `V2.SsKey` recovery in `ReadSide.buildAttribute`** (only `recoverKindSsKey` exists today, `:992`; the attribute equivalent is the one genuinely-new engine piece, deferred until a live two-DB flow demands it).

**Deferred as CLI/config surface (not on the canary path):** **M1** ŌĆö the `rendition: physical | logical` env flag (`Substrate`/`resolveFlowSpec`); and **M3.b** ŌĆö routing a `legacy` flow to `runWithRenames` from a shared model rendered in both renditions. Both follow the proven engine capability, exactly as M1's env flag was unneeded for M2's canary. *(Both landed the following slice ŌĆö see the next entry.)*

---

## 2026-06-09 ŌĆö Cloud-insertion surface slices: M1 (rendition flag) / G0c (advisory survey) / G0b (P10 probe) / M3.b (reverse-leg face)

Four surface slices on the cloud-insertion layer, each a separate green commit. The engine capabilities (Transfer, reconcile, `runWithRenames`, the capability survey's 3 probe axes) already existed; these wire the operator-facing surface around them under R6.

**M1 ŌĆö the `rendition: physical | logical` env-metadata flag (config surface).** Added a closed DU `Rendition = Physical | Logical` and an `Environment.Rendition : Rendition option` field, parsed from a `rendition` config key by `ProjectionConfig.parse` (`MovementSurface.fs`). Default absent ŌćÆ `None` ŌĆö the minimal non-breaking default; the established same-rendition surface never sets it. Env METADATA, not a refusal gate: it does not narrow `access`/`grant`; it marks whether a place bears the physical OSUSR cloud rendition (A) or the logical on-prem rendition (B) of the one authored `SsKey` model, so the reverse-leg wiring can pick source=logical / sink=physical. Unknown value ŌćÆ `cli.config.envRenditionUnknown` (named, never dropped). Documented in `THE_CLI.md` ┬¦4.1. No engine behavior change (the LE-2 canary proves the engine capability).

**G0c ŌĆö advisory survey surfacing in the run verbs (R6 warn-not-stop).** Before a live Execute, the run-flow path (`PlanAction.Transfer` ŌåÆ `runTransfer`) surfaces any blocked-capability / unreachable survey findings over the touched environments as a STDERR ADVISORY WARNING, then proceeds regardless ŌĆö the run's own exit stands. The standalone `survey` verb keeps its exit-7 hard-stop unchanged; the in-flow path does NOT borrow that exit. `CapabilitySurvey.blocked` is now the single predicate the standalone verb's gate AND the in-flow advisory both read for the MESSAGE (they differ only on EXIT); `CapabilitySurvey.advisoryLines` (pure, `string list`) builds the warning text. The survey is computed at the dispatch layer (where `cfg` is in scope; `discoverConfig` sits below `runTransfer` in `Program.fs`) and threaded into `runTransfer`; only a `--go` Flow carries an advisory (preview / non-flow verbs carry the empty list, byte-identical). This is the S3 posture made concrete: per R6 (`DECISIONS 2026-06-09 ŌĆö capability survey S3 posture`), V2 owns no production write path during dual-track, so the gate is advisory until the per-pair V2-driver flip.

**G0b ŌĆö the P10 user-directory-readability survey probe (report field, totality preserved).** Added `ReadSide.userDirectoryReadability` (modeled on `cdcTrackedTables`): reports whether a platform user-directory table is SELECT-able (`Found`) and whether it exposes an email-shaped key column (`EmailKeyed`) ŌĆö the FK target `golden`/`preview` re-key against by email. Surfaced as a NEW FIELD `EnvironmentReport.UserDirectory`, **NOT a `Capability` DU variant** ŌĆö a variant would break the `required Ōćö surveyed` totality (`CapabilitySurveyTotalityTests`, which stays green untouched). Populated in `probeEnvironment` next to the `cdcTrackedTables` call; rendered in `buildSurveyView`. The candidate user-table set is operator-configurable (the probe takes the names; conventional fallback `OSSYS_USER`/`User`/`USERS`). **Residual (OPEN-2):** the real OutSystems user-table identity is instance-specific; the OFFLINE Docker witness (`UserDirectoryProbeTests`) deploys a stand-in and proves the probe SEMANTICS ŌĆö real-instance validation pairs with OPEN-2.

**M3.b ŌĆö the `legacy` reverse-leg flow-recognition FACE.** The engine + canary half (LE-2) was proven green by the prior M3/LE-2 entry above (the two-contract `runWithRenames` path IS the mechanism; the prior entry's "M3.b deferred" is now picked up here). This slice adds the operator-facing FACE on top: `Command.reverseLegOf` (pure, tested) recognizes a flow as the BŌåÆA reverse leg from the M1 rendition flag ŌĆö `Some` exactly when a live `logical` source (B) moves to a live `physical` sink (A); resolves the two connection specs. `TransferRun.runReverseLeg` is the thin engine face that delegates to the LE-2-proven `Transfer.runWithRenames` GIVEN the two contracts. **The per-flow runner ARM is deliberately not forced** ŌĆö it needs the two SsKey-aligned contracts (logical source / physical sink), which the LE-2 canary supplies as authored stable-SsKey contracts; for a real live two-DB flow, ReadSide SsKeys are name-derived (two independent reads do not align), so producing the contracts needs either a shared authored model rendered in both renditions (the migration-team flow) or attribute-scope `V2.SsKey` recovery in `ReadSide.buildAttribute` (the durable, larger piece). The classifier + the engine face are the landed, tested partial; the runner arm is the remaining wiring. Documented in `THE_DATA_PRODUCERS.md` ┬¦6 LE-1 + `CONFIRMED_BACKLOG` J3.

No existing test was weakened; no refusal's exit code or precedence was changed. Pure pool green; the G0b Docker witness green against the warm container. Status surfaces updated: `PREFLIGHT_CLOUD_INSERTION.md` M1 / M3 / M4 (all Ō£ģ); `CONFIRMED_BACKLOG` J1/J3/J4.

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö The config unification closed (A44 Ōłģ): one isomorphic `projection.json`; the three operator decisions wired; the LogicalTableEmission clobber fixed

The S1ŌĆōS6 build of `THE_CONFIG_CONTROL_PLANE.md` is complete: the two historically-disjoint config surfaces ŌĆö **movement** (`ProjectionConfig`: `environments`/`flows`) and **model-shaping** (`Config`: `model`/`overrides`/`emission`/`policy`/ŌĆ”) ŌĆö are now **one isomorphic `projection.json` behind two views**, and the engine's resolved `MovementSpec` is a total, faithful, direction-derived image of it. This entry records the resolved questions of the unification's final slice (S6); the slice plan and per-gap close are in `THE_CONFIG_CONTROL_PLANE.md` ┬¦3/┬¦7/┬¦8.

**The law and its canary.** The unification's forcing function is the A44 `expressible Ōćö reachable` law (`tests/Projection.Tests/MovementIsomorphismTests.fs`, S5), the movement-space instance of the iso-ladder ŌĆö sibling of the capability survey's `required Ōćö surveyed` and the transform registry's `registered Ōćö executed`. Three clauses: (1) **faithful** ŌĆö `parse Ōłś render = id` on the movement config DOM (the `renderFlow`/`renderEnvironment` inverse ╬© over a constructed-valid generator); (2) **total/spanning** ŌĆö every model-bearing `PlanAction` the engine consumes has a flow pre-image, and every parse-accepted flow resolves to a real action or a NAMED refusal; (3) **direction derived** ŌĆö `MovementSpec.Direction` is a pure function of `(srcRendition, sinkRendition, origin)`, never a stored knob. The canary's value is the partition: `mustReachActions ŌŖÄ residualActions` is the whole model-bearing surface, so a residual cannot silently widen. **After S6 the residual set is Ōłģ** ŌĆö `residualActions = Set.empty`, the strongest A44 statement (every model-bearing arm flow-reachable). The two prior `[<Fact(Skip=ŌĆ”)>]` residual stubs are flipped to `[<Fact>]`.

**Three operator decisions, how each was wired:**

1. **Wire `ModelSource.ConfigFile` into flows (G3, S6.2) ŌĆö trigger = store presence.** The publish-with-provenance arms (`PublishBundle` folder / `PublishAndLoad` live `--go`; both carry `store ├Ś env`) fire only on `ModelSource.ConfigFile`, which `resolveFlowSpec` never emitted (the dead wire ŌĆö every `projection <flow>` ran with `Policy.empty`, silently dropping the shaping). The locked trigger: emit `ConfigFile` exactly when the flow targets a **provenance-bearing place** ŌĆö the config has a load provenance (the new `ProjectionConfig.SourcePath : string option`, set by `fromFile`, `None` for `parse`/`empty`; it is load provenance, NOT a rendered JSON field, so `parse Ōłś render` ignores it), the sink carries a `store`, and a `model` path is present. Every store-less target and every from-string config keeps the byte-identical `ModelFile`/`Unspecified` path, so the empty/default-config invariant holds. **Runner completeness:** the path is real end-to-end ŌĆö `runPlan` resolves the `config` path through the unified loader (`runFullExport`/`runFullExportLoad` ŌåÆ `FullExportRun.execute*` / `Compose.runWithConfigAndLoad`), so the overlays + provenance/episode store flow in; it is not a stub.

2. **Add a flow `shape` field (S6.1) ŌĆö make `EmitSkeleton` flow-expressible.** The flow vocabulary had no `shape`, so a folder model flow always landed on `EmitBundle`; the skeleton (pre-overlay) emit was reachable only via `osm emit --skeleton-only`, outside the flow resolver. Added `Flow.Shape : Shape option` (`"bundle"` | `"ssdt"` | `"skeleton"`; `None` = the `Bundle` default, byte-identical), parsed by `parseFlowShape` (mirroring `parseFlowScope` ŌĆö named refusal `cli.config.flowShapeUnknown`), rendered by `renderShape` only when `Some` (the default round-trips through the absent arm), resolved to `MovementSpec.Shape`. A folder flow with `"shape": "skeleton"` now lands on `EmitSkeleton`.

3. **Fix the LogicalTableEmission physical-`tableRenames` clobber (S6.3).** The defect: `applyRenames` (the operator's `TableRename` specs) runs BEFORE the registered pass chain; the chain's `LogicalTableEmission` then re-derives `Kind.Physical.Table` from the logical `Name`, **clobbering** an operator's physical-form override (`{ "from": { schema, table }, "to": ŌĆ” }`) ŌĆö so a physical-form `tableRenames` entry was a no-op in emitted DDL (the logical name won), contradicting `LogicalTableEmission`'s own "operator pins dominate" docstring contract. Fix: `LogicalTableEmission` SKIPS kinds an operator physically pinned. The pinned SsKeys are resolved from the physical-form rename `from` keys against the **original** catalog (the OSSYS coordinate the operator named ŌĆö a post-emission match would miss, which is the clobber) and threaded through a pins-carrying chain sibling (`RegisteredTransforms.chainStepsWithPins` / `allChainStepsForWithPins`; `Pipeline.projectWithStateWithPins`) at the three overlay seams (`runWithConfigCore` / `projectWithConfig` / `applyShapingToCatalog`). `Set.empty` is the byte-identical default everywhere ŌĆö the registered-chain source of truth, the `registered Ōćö executed` metadata, and the many `projectWithState` callers are unchanged; only physical-form renames are pinned (logical-form behavior unchanged). The Operator-reality canary stays green (empty diff): no byte change to unrelated emissions.

**Per-flow `shaping` override (S6.4) ŌĆö whole-section granularity.** Per the operator's earlier "global + opt-in per-flow override" answer: `Flow.Shaping : Config.Config option` (parsed from a nested `"shaping"` object via `Config.parseLenient`; `None` = the global, byte-identical) deep-overlays the global `cfg.Shaping` for that flow's emission via `Config.overlay`. A **faithful field-level deep merge is deferred** as more than one slice of work; the override is implemented at **whole-section granularity** ŌĆö for each of the nine top-level shaping sections, the flow's section wins iff it differs from the lenient default (the flow authored it), else the global's section holds. An override equal to `defaultConfig` is the identity, so a silent/empty override is byte-identical to the global. The effective shaping is computed at the CLI dispatch (`effectiveShaping`) and threaded into `runPlan`. (A field-level merge is the natural follow-on when a flow needs to override one field of a section the global also sets; the section-granularity form is the minimal opt-in that the singular-control-plane requirement demanded.)

**Consumers + scaffold + docs.** The `explain node/suggest/policy` verbs already resolve through the unified loader (`Config.fromFile` over the same `projection.json` document), so no rewire was needed. `projection init` now scaffolds the unified shape (the canonical `model` object, an `emission` block, environments with a `store`, and a `skeleton` flow example) rather than the pre-unification movement-only `modelOssys` form. `CONFIG_REFERENCE.md`'s "two separate surfaces / shaping is not layered onto flow emission" claim ŌĆö now FALSE ŌĆö is rewritten to describe the one control plane; `examples/projection.sample.json` + `README.md` gained `shape`/`shaping`/store examples on the cloud-insertion estate; `THE_CONFIG_CONTROL_PLANE.md` ┬¦3 G3 + ┬¦7 mark the residuals RESOLVED.

Each sub-slice shipped as its own green commit. Pure pool green (`scripts/test.sh fast`); the A44 canary + the Operator-reality canary green warm. No load-bearing commitment was broken; the byte-identical-default invariant holds at every new seam (`SourcePath`/`Shape`/`Shaping` absent, store-less targets, `Set.empty` pins, `defaultConfig` overlay).

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö The remaining-shelf sweep: A3 delete-scope semantics; the dacpac wire + the publish-equivalence witness; D8/J2 surface decisions; test-runner observability

Six wires closed the A-cluster residuals (`CONFIRMED_BACKLOG` ┬¦Ōō¬ŌĆ▓ carries the
per-commit ledger). The resolved questions, durable beyond the slice:

**A3 ŌĆö per-kind delete-scope resolution is faithful omission, not a skip.**
The convergent-delete scope (`emission.deleteScope { terms: [{column,
value}] }` ŌåÆ Core `DeleteScopePolicy` on the Emission axis) resolves PER KIND
(`DeleteScopePolicy.resolveFor`): the `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE ŌĆ” THEN
DELETE` arm renders exactly when every term column is an attribute of the
kind. A kind that does not carry the gating column has NO row inside the
scope ŌĆö the predicate selects nothing there ŌĆö so omitting the arm is the
faithful rendering of the scope's semantics, not a silent partial
application. This is why the binding does not refuse on mixed estates (a
tenant gate over an estate with non-tenant tables is well-formed). Layering:
the scope's raw terms are typed per kind at render via the same
`SqlLiteral.ofRaw` lift row values ride (no single shared literal ŌĆö column
types may differ across kinds); the composer threads the scope as a PLAIN
VALUE (`EmissionPolicy.DeleteScope` read once at the dispatch layer), so the
emitters never see `Policy` and A18 holds. Witnesses:
`StaticSeedsEmitterTests` AC-D7 cluster (scoped arm with per-kind typed
literal; case-insensitive physical-column match; missing-column kind keeps
the upsert-only MERGE; `None` byte-identical).

**The dacpac wire ŌĆö an inert default flips OFF when its gate gains effect.**
`emission.dacpac` parsed but was never honored; the wire (the `.dacpac`
compiled in `runWithConfigCore` over the SAME emitted catalog the SSDT step
projects, staged as `projection.dacpac` in the atomic write) would have
grown every default bundle had the default stayed `true`. Decision: a config
gate that was inert at `true` flips to `false` in the commit that makes it
effective ŌĆö the observable default output stays byte-identical and the
explicit opt-in is the only way the artifact set grows. (The general form of
the byte-identical-default invariant for wiring previously-dead flags.)

**The DacFx/ScriptDom "ALTER drift" question is CLOSED ŌĆö boundary, not
drift; now witnessed live.** The 2026-06-10 review flagged dual ALTER
renderers as the one open architectural risk. Investigation: the split is
the DOCUMENTED decision boundary (`DacpacEmitter` header; `WAVE_6_ONTOLOGY`
┬¦4) ŌĆö the `.dacpac` is the declarative model whose deployment plan DacFx
computes at publish; the migrate verb's ScriptDom ALTERs are the imperative
in-place executor; the emitter excludes imperative DDL from the package by
construction. What was genuinely missing: no test ever published the package
through `DacServices.Deploy` against live SQL Server (coverage stopped at
the in-memory `TSqlModel`). `DacpacPublishEquivalenceTests` (Docker pool)
closes the leg: one Catalog ŌåÆ the deployed bundle vs the published dacpac ŌåÆ
both read back ŌåÆ `PhysicalSchema` equality (deployed-vs-deployed, so shared
erasures cancel). Green on the IDENTITY/FK/unique-index/nullability
envelope; a future divergence between the two publish paths fails the
Docker pool. Do not re-open the "unify the ALTER surface" framing ŌĆö the
witness is the contract.

**D8 ŌĆö the synthesis knobs are `--seed <n>` / `--scale <f>`,** per
THE_SYNTHETIC_DATA_DESIGN ┬¦7's vocabulary (volume is a FACTOR over the
profiled `RowCount`, not an absolute row count; the backlog's "--rows N"
phrasing was loose). Per-run intent (`FlowRunOpts`), never config; malformed
values are named refusals; on a non-synthetic action the knobs are noted,
never silently dropped.

**J2 ŌĆö per-flow `"reconcile": ["<table>:<match-column>"]`** makes the golden
re-key declaratively expressible. Entries are parse-validated through the
SAME `TransferSpec.parseReconcileSpec` contract the `--reconcile` tail
proves (one validation surface, two entry points), rendered by the
declarative dual (the A44 `parse Ōłś render = id` law extends to the field),
threaded via `resolveFlowSpec` into the transfer leg's `LoadOpts`.

**Smaller closures:** A1's migrate residual (the migrate pre-flights route
through the one mandatory `Preflight.all`, the permission gate sequenced on
the connection gate's hot task so two probes never share the one
`SqlConnection` concurrently); `shape: manifest` (the manifest as a
standalone artifact via a single-file write that leaves a published bundle
standing ŌĆö deliberately NOT the bundle's atomic directory replace); B8 (the
physical-table and attribute-ref lookups centralize in `CatalogResolution`
under its lookup-returns-option contract).

**Test-runner observability (amends `DECISIONS 2026-06-09 ŌĆö Agent
test-execution protocol`).** The recurring stall was a pool run launched in
the background (or piped through `tail`, which buffers to EOF) going dark
with no cheap question to ask. Every `run_pool` now tees its stream to
`<pool>.live.log` and maintains a one-line `<pool>.status` (`RUNNING pid=ŌĆ”`
ŌåÆ `PASSED`/`FAILED exit=ŌĆ” failed=<names>`; a signal trap writes `KILLED` so
a dead run cannot masquerade as in-flight); **`scripts/test.sh status`** is
the one-command answer: verdict, pid liveness, live-log staleness (>120s
flags the warm-container wedge with the `warm-sql.sh restart` hint), last
output lines. Launch pools bare in the background; poll `status`; never
pipe a long run.

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö A7 polarity RESOLVED: the module-filter include flags stay opt-in

Operator decision (this session): `model.includeSystemModules` /
`includeInactiveModules` keep today's opt-in semantics ŌĆö effective only
alongside a non-empty `model.modules` selection. The estate-wide form
(flags filtering with no modules named) was considered and declined; it
remains available as a future deliberate default-behavior change should a
real config demand it, but it is no longer an open question. The
inert combination is no longer silent (same-day wire:
`ModuleFilterBinding.inertFlagNote` ŌåÆ the dispatch Note channel + the
`moduleFilter.flagsInert` Info diagnostic), so the opt-in gate is now
named at the operator surface rather than discovered by absence. Closes
the `CONFIRMED_BACKLOG` "STILL OPEN ŌĆö A7 polarity" row.

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö J3 residual CLOSED: the reverse-leg contract source is the ONE authored model rendered at both renditions (`CatalogRendition`)

The BŌåÆA `legacy` reverse leg's last gap was the contract source: `Transfer.runReverseLeg`
(M3.b) needed two SsKey-ALIGNED `Catalog` contracts ŌĆö the logical on-prem source (B) and
the physical OSUSR cloud sink (A) ŌĆö and a live two-DB flow could not produce them
(`ReadSide` synthesizes attribute SsKeys from physical coordinates, so two independent
reads never align by identity). **Decision: option (1) ŌĆö render the one authored model at
both renditions.** `CatalogRendition.logical` runs the SAME two emission-axis passes the
down-leg publish applies (`LogicalTableEmission` + `LogicalColumnEmission`, `Enabled` ŌĆö
the production default), so the B contract names tables/columns exactly as the published
bundle deployed them; `CatalogRendition.physical` is the authored catalog (the identity,
named for call-site legibility). Both passes preserve `SsKey` (A1), so the pair aligns BY
CONSTRUCTION. A load-bearing property fell out: the logical `Name`s are rendition-invariant,
so the BŌåÆA rename map is the IDENTITY ŌĆö the rendition difference rides each contract's
physical coordinates at its own SQL boundary (the LE-2-proven mechanism), tested as such.

**Option (2) ŌĆö live attribute-scope `V2.SsKey` recovery in `ReadSide.buildAttribute` ŌĆö
was NOT pursued** (it needs a new column-level extended-property persistence surface on
emission plus a read path: ~100 LOC + a metadata contract, for no gain while a model is
present). Re-open trigger: a reverse leg over an estate with NO authored model.

The wiring: `PlanAction.RunReverseLeg` now carries the model (`PreviewSchema`'s
convention); a model-less legacy flow refuses at PLAN time (`cli.move.modelMissing`,
exit 1) ŌĆö the old runtime `cli.move.reverseLegResidual` refusal (exit 6) is retired.
`Transfer.runReverseLegThroughConnections` drives the leg through the `TransferConnections`
apparatus (D9) with the full write-options envelope (emission / resumable / table subset
resolved against the source contract by rendition-invariant `Name`). The CLI face
(`runReverseLegTransfer`) mirrors the peer transfer's gates (execute env-gate ŌåÆ 7; the
G0c advisory survey channel) and REFUSES reconcile/rekey on the reverse leg
(`transfer.reverseLeg.reconcileUnsupported`, exit 2) ŌĆö the reconcile + rename combination
stays the named follow-on; never a silent straight-load. The S3 module filter applies to
the model ONCE (`needCatalog`), so both renditions narrow identically; flow `shaping`
overlays do NOT shape reverse-leg contracts (the leg moves data against the estates as
the model names them).

Witness: `M3/LE-1 ŌĆ” contracts RENDERED from the one authored model (CatalogRendition)
round-trip` (Docker) ŌĆö the rendered logical contract is deployed + seeded as B, piped up
into the as-authored physical A, and the OSUSR sink's CONTACT column carries the source's
Email data. Pure: rendition substitution + SsKey-alignment + identity-repoint tests; the
plan-time model gate; the A44 clause-2/3 canaries hold (the spanning estate carries a
model). Two-consumer extraction fired in `RunFaces`: the 6.A.1 drop-narration block is now
`narrateDropExit`, shared by the peer and reverse-leg faces. Also fixed: `scripts/test.sh`
failed-name extraction used `grep -B1` on the TRX and could blame the textually-preceding
(passed) test; testName and outcome share one line, so the context flag is gone.

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö 6.A.2 LIFTED (operator-authorized): cyclic AssignedBySink loads via a phase-2 re-point keyed on the ASSIGNED PK; the set-based capture lane replaces per-row INSERTŌĆ”OUTPUT

Two engine changes on the transfer write path, driven by the operator's
288M-row reverse-leg estate (Ōēż4h window; huge FK-referenced tables; lift
authorized; chunk-level resume requested ŌĆö the resume model remains open,
see the LE-3 report addendum).

**(1) The 6.A.2 refusal is lifted.** `transfer.cyclicAssignedBySink`
(DECISIONS 6.A.2; `executeGate`) refused any `AssignedBySink` kind carrying
cycle-deferred FK columns because `phase2UpdateSql` keyed its WHERE on the
source PK the sink had replaced. The fix the refusal itself named is now
built: Phase 1 re-points EXCLUDING the deferred columns (their targets'
captures are incomplete mid-kind; resolving early would wrongly drop rows)
and inserts them as NULL; Phase 2 re-points against the COMPLETED remap and,
for `AssignedBySink` kinds, translates each row's PK through the captured
remap so the UPDATE keys on the ASSIGNED PK. A deferred FK value with no
captured target is a NAMED phase-2 erasure (the row stands, the reference is
lost, surfaced in `SkippedReferences` ŌåÆ exit 9), never a silent NULL. A row
whose own PK has no capture was dropped (and named) in phase 1 and is passed
over. Witnesses: the rewritten 6.A.2 canary (source keys at 1000+, the
manager chain joined by NAME, a deliberate FORWARD reference so a
phase-1-only re-point cannot pass), the reverse-leg self-FK canary (the
orphan-manager named erasure), the refusal-totality property (the lifted
shape gates None), and the pure execute-gate pin. The self-referencing
IDENTITY shape (`User.ManagerId`, `Employee.CreatedBy`) no longer refuses
the estate.

**(2) The capture lane is set-based.** Per-row `INSERT ŌĆ” OUTPUT` measured
~271 rows/sec end-to-end (Tier-4 bench, warm container) ŌĆö 12+ days for
288M rows. The `VALUES`-form MERGE measured ~5.7k rows/sec, parse-bound at
the table-value constructor's 1000-row cap. The shipped lane, per 50k-row
chunk: bulk-copy into a session-scoped staging table whose columns are
cloned FROM THE SINK TABLE (`SELECT TOP 0 ŌĆ” INTO`; **ISNULL strips the
IDENTITY property through SELECT INTO ŌĆö a CASE wrapper does NOT: it
constant-folds and the property propagates, silently minting staging keys;
probed live 2026-06-10**), then ONE `MERGE ŌĆ” OUTPUT S.[__SRC_KEY],
INSERTED.<pk>` per chunk. tempdb rights are implicit for every principal,
so the lane fits the DML-only `grant: data` envelope (the DML-only
principal canary proves it). Sibling lane: an `AssignedBySink` kind no FK
targets has no remap consumer and rides `Bulk.copyRowsSinkMinted`
(KeepNulls only ŌĆö no KeepIdentity, no implicit ALTER). Measured: ~27k
rows/sec sustained at 100k rows ŌćÆ 288M Ōēł 3.0h, inside the window, under
the worst-case all-referenced shape. A36 holds: realization-layer swap,
same `SurrogateRemapContext` feed, no IR change.

Re-open triggers: platform triggers on real OSUSR tables force the
`OUTPUT INTO` form (survey P5); a real-wire bench materially below ~20k
rows/sec re-opens chunk sizing / parallel per-table loading; the remap's
in-memory Map (string-keyed) at hundreds of millions of FK-target rows
re-opens the packed-int64 / sink-resident keymap design (named open ŌĆö
the streaming + chunk-resume program, LE-3 report addendum ┬¦scale).

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö The streaming realization + chunk-resume journal: bounded memory for the 288M-row straight load; the journal is CLIENT-side because the sink is DML-only

**The decision.** `Transfer.runStreamingWithRenames` is the bounded-memory
realization of the straight-load (non-reconciling, Incremental) transfer:
the plan is built STRUCTURE-ONLY (order, dispositions, deferred columns ŌĆö
rows never enter it), and each kind's rows stream from the source in
`CaptureChunkSize` chunks through rename-repoint ŌåÆ FK-repoint (deferred
excluded) ŌåÆ the capture ladder / minted-bulk / preserved-bulk lanes. Only
the packed remap and the chunk in flight are resident ŌĆö the materialized
path's `Ingestion.collectInOrder` (whole-estate `Map<SsKey, StaticRow
list>`) is untenable at 288M rows. Phase 2 re-STREAMS the deferred kinds
against the completed remap; its UPDATEs are idempotent, so a resumed run
repeating them is harmless. `AsyncStream.nextBatch` is re-introduced under
this consumer (the Wave-0 slice 0.2 retirement's named trigger fired). The
reconcile and WipeAndLoad envelopes stay on the materialized path ŌĆö the
named follow-ons, never a silent half-support.

**The journal (chunk-level resume, operator-chosen).** `CaptureJournal` is
an append-only NDJSON ledger, one file per (directory, plan marker), one
`ChunkRecord` per completed chunk: the chunk's SOURCE fingerprint
(first/last PK + raw count ŌĆö deterministic because `ReadSide` orders by
PK), the written count, and the chunk's captured pairs. It lives
CLIENT-SIDE by necessity, not preference: the cloud sink is `grant: data`,
so the G10 progress table's `CREATE TABLE` is exactly what the grant
forbids ŌĆö the engine's own ledger is the D9-consistent home. Semantics:
a journaled chunk whose fingerprint matches is SKIPPED and its pairs
rebuild the remap (the resumed run re-points later kinds at the FIRST
run's minted keys); a mismatch is the named `transfer.resume.sourceDrift`
refusal ŌĆö never a silent re-run over changed data; a COMPLETED run re-runs
as a full skip ŌĆö the streaming path's idempotent re-run, closing the G3
duplicate hazard whenever a journal is supplied. The chunk's sink
statement (one MERGE / one bulk batch) is the atomic commit point: a crash
DURING a chunk never journals it; a crash AFTER the append never
re-executes it. Witnesses: streaming equivalence against the LE-3
keystone (joins, minted keys, named orphan drop, exit 9); the
crash-resume canary (sink table missing mid-load ŌåÆ remediate ŌåÆ resume:
no duplicates, remap rebuilt from journal); the idempotent re-run canary;
the source-drift refusal canary.

Re-open triggers: reconcile Ōłś streaming (the cloud-owns-its-users reverse
leg at scale); WipeAndLoad Ōłś journal (the wipe must invalidate the
journal); journal compaction (288M pairs Ōēł a multi-GB NDJSON ŌĆö acceptable
for a cutover run, named here); the resumed run's report counts the
resumed run's work (the journaled run reported its own drops ŌĆö exit-9
semantics are per-run; revisit if the operator wants a merged ledger).

## 2026-06-10 ŌĆö Capability-descent is the house pattern for sink-capability-sensitive write seams (operator-endorsed); deployment is conservative by three rules

The operator endorsed generalizing the capture-lane ladder ("reverse
progressive enhancement") as a performance-aware pattern, deployed
conservatively. Codified: capability descent applies at any write seam
whose preferred realization can be REFUSED BY THE SINK for a capability
reason (triggers, grant shape, feature availability) ŌĆö lanes as separate
functions, a closed DU naming the rungs, ONE orchestrator descending one
rung at a time, every descent named on the run's report. Three deployment
rules, none negotiable without an amendment: (1) the capability-error
recognizer never widens casually ŌĆö each SQL error number that triggers
descent is argued and canary-tested like 334 was; (2) a DATA error
(constraint violation, conversion) always propagates ŌĆö degrading on data
errors masks corruption; (3) every new rung lands with a canary proving
behavioral equivalence on the degraded lane. The ladder is already
deployed at the one seam with live capability variance (the transfer
write path, shared by every transfer entry point); future
capability-sensitive seams adopt the pattern rather than branching inside
a lane. Per the two-consumer threshold, no speculative generalization of
the orchestrator into a generic combinator until a second seam exists.

Wiring + bench (same session): `--streaming` / `--journal <dir>` thread
through `FlowRunOpts` ŌåÆ `MovementSpec` ŌåÆ `LoadOpts` ŌåÆ the reverse-leg CLI
face, which refuses the unsupported combinations by name
(`transfer.reverseLeg.streamingTablesUnsupported` /
`.streamingResumableUnsupported` / `.streamingWipeUnsupported` /
`.journalRequiresStreaming`); the apparatus entry is
`Transfer.runStreamingReverseLegThroughConnections`. The streaming bench:
~32.5k rows/sec bare; ONE-CHUNK INGEST PREFETCH (chunk N+1 pulls from the
source while chunk N writes to the sink ŌĆö hot task, distinct connections)
lifted it to a stable ~35.5k rows/sec (288M Ōēł 2.3h, loopback) ŌĆö kept per
the bench protocol; the overlap's value grows with real network latency.

## 2026-06-11 ŌĆö The reverse-leg realization SELECTOR: the dominant realization is chosen automatically; flags are overrides, downgrades are never silent

The operator asked whether the realization choice is "inherently dynamic
so it arrives at the best possible realization across all applicable use
cases." Resolution: where one realization DOMINATES on every measured
axis for the requests it admits, dynamism is a DEFAULT WITH NAMED
FALLBACK CONDITIONS, not a heuristic chooser. `ReverseLegRealization.
choose` (pure, total, connection-free) selects the STREAMING realization
automatically whenever the request admits it (Incremental, no table
subset, no --resumable) ŌĆö it is faster (~35.5k vs ~27k rows/sec at the
2026-06-10/11 bench), bounded-memory, and journal-resumable ŌĆö and falls
back to the materialized path for the combinations streaming does not
yet support. `--journal` alone rides the auto-selected stream. An
EXPLICIT `--streaming` on an inadmissible combination refuses by name
(never a silent downgrade); a journal on an inadmissible combination
likewise. The genuinely dynamic layers stay dynamic: the per-kind lane
choice (capture vs minted-bulk, by FK-target analysis) and the
capability-descent ladder. Re-open triggers: streaming gains table-subset
/ wipe support (the fallback conditions shrink toward empty and the
selector arms retire); a measured regime where materialized beats
streaming (none observed ŌĆö would re-open the dominance premise).
